summary: Uncle Bob presents a fictional dialogue among developers processing a new CTO's demands for professional behavior and higher standards. The team initially bristles at being called unprofessional, but gradually acknowledges their role in shipping poor quality software by always saying 'yes' and never pushing back on unrealistic deadlines. Through their conversation, they recognize that their habits of rushing, avoiding code cleanup out of fear, and making empty promises have created the dysfunction. The post ends with the team accepting the challenge and asking how to actually implement the CTO's expectations.
key_points: Developers often contribute to dysfunction by saying 'yes' to everything and promising to 'try' rather than giving honest estimates
Fear of breaking code prevents developers from cleaning it, creating a downward spiral of quality
The mantra 'the only way to go fast is to go well' challenges the false dichotomy between speed and quality
QA finding nothing should be the goal, not an impossible standard - developers own quality
Professional behavior means telling the truth and sticking to it, even when managers pressure for different answers
money_quotes: Maybe the reason we've been shipping shit is because we've been saying 'Yes' all the time.
The Only Way To Go Fast, Is To Go Well!
We don't clean our code, because we're afraid that if we do we'll break it, and then we'll be in trouble.
How are we going to get anything done on time if we have to do it right all the time.
We're always promising to try, as though that's going to change anything.
Maybe we should just tell them the truth and stick to it.
key_insight: Shipping poor quality software is often a self-inflicted wound caused by developers who say 'yes' to unrealistic demands instead of professionally asserting the truth about what's achievable.
